steps.title: Separate reputation and conduct
steps.content: The text first reports how others view Mr. Alden. The laity regard him as powerful and learned, and his fellow clergy regard him as discreet and agreeable. After establishing that favorable reputation, the text shifts to the habits that help him keep it.

steps.title: Connect habits to opinion
steps.content: Mr. Alden avoids warm discussion, suggests what might be thought instead of plainly saying what he thinks, and prevents people from seeing that he is laughing at them. Those behaviors explain how he avoids conflict or ridicule and continues to appear agreeable, discreet, and impressive to other people.

steps.title: Reject unsupported arcs
steps.content: Choice A invents a contrast between Bellford and London. Choice C claims his reputation has declined, but the text does not show a fall in others' regard. Choice D invents a hidden private self. Choice B matches the structure: favorable opinion first, then behavior that sustains it. The answer is B.
